content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 17, 1993

Poppy DeMarco Dennis
4690 North Lane
Del Mar, CA 92014

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-141

Dear Ms. Dennis:

You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTIONS

You own the Bea M. Sweeney Memorial Tech Center (the
"Center") which prints voter walk or call lists and mailing
labels. You have asked the following questions concerning that
business:

1. In order to avoid making contributions to candidates who
purchase materials from the Center, how much must the Center
charge for its services? Must the Center's prices be comparable
to those of large data houses? What if the labor is donated by
the person producing the materials?

2. Will it make a difference if the Center changes its
business status and forms a cooperative or a 501(c)(4)
organization?

3. If the Democratic Central Committee purchases materials
from the Center and sells the materials to candidates, how much
must the Committee charge?

CONCLUSIONS

1. A contribution is made if goods or services are provided
to a candidate for which the candidate has not provided full and
adequate consideration. The value of a non-monetary contribution

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Poppy DeMarco Dennis
Our File No. A-93-141
Page 2


is its "fair market value," i.e., the amount the candidate would
pay on the open market for the same or equivalent goods or
services. Therefore, if the candidate pays the fair market value
for the Center's services, no contribution will occur. To
determine the fair market value, you may use the prices charged by
businesses of similar size for such services. If one of the
Center's employees wishes to donate his or her labor to a
particular candidate, the Center may reduce the price by the cost
of labor.

     2.    Forming a cooperative or a 501(c)(4) organization will
not change this conclusion.

     3.    You have not indicated that you are authorized to
request advice on behalf of the Democratic Central Committee.
Therefore, we cannot provide advice concerning the Committee's
duties under the Act. However, the answer to Question 1 and the
following analysis should provide some guidance.

                              FACTS

     The Center is a relatively new business established for the
purpose of printing voter walk or call lists and mailing labels.
The Center has very little overhead and is able to produce the
materials inexpensively. Some data is obtained from the
Democratic Central Committee in exchange for the Center keeping
the raw data. You plan to charge $7 per 1,000 for walk lists and
$8 per 1,000 for labels. Theoretically, this price would cover
the Center's rent, labor, depreciation, and provide a small
profit.

     Market prices for these services vary tremendously. Large
data houses range from $13 per 1,000 for large runs to $32 per
1,000 for short runs. Smaller consultants have informed you that
they have been able to obtain the services from other local
sources for $7-$8 per 1,000.

                              ANALYSIS

     A contribution is a payment for political purposes for which
full and adequate consideration is not received. (Section 82015.)
The term "payment" includes "...services or anything of value,
whether tangible or intangible." (Section 82044.)

     The standard for valuing non-monetary contributions to
candidates is set forth in Section 82025.5, which states:

          Whenever the amount of goods, services,
     facilities, or anything of value other than money
     is required to be reported under this title, the
     amount reported shall be the fair market value...."

Poppy DeMarco Dennis
Our File No. A-93-141
Page 3


     The fair market value is the amount a candidate would have to
pay to acquire similar goods or services on the open market.
(White Advice Letter, No. A-77-018.) To the extent your prices
will cover the Center's costs, provide a profit, and you have
determined they are comparable to prices charged by similar small
businesses, you may assume that you are receiving full and
adequate consideration from the candidates who purchase goods or
services from the Center. You should also take into account the
amount you would charge if a non-candidate entity wished to
purchase the same or similar goods or services from the
Center.

     Finally, the term "contribution" specifically excludes
volunteer personal services. (Section 82015.) Therefore, if
employees of the Center wish to donate their labor to produce
lists for a particular candidate, you may reduce the charges by
the amount normally charged for labor.

     I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. If you have
additional questions, please do not hesitate to contact me at
(916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Jeff Marschner
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature: Carla Wardlow]

                                        By: Carla Wardlow
                                            Division Chief
                                            Technical Assistance Division
